title: America’s $205 Billion Government Fund You’ve Never Heard Of | Conor Coleman, Head of Investments at Development Finance Corporation (DFC)

segments.text: Today's episode is brought to you by the Tukurium Corn Fund ticker C O R N. Let's get into it.
There's a very, very large player in global finance that almost no one knows about.
The development finance corporation, it just had its reauthorization from $60 billion to $205
billion. A lot of people talk about BlackRock, Federal Reserve, and yes,
the numbers there are very large as well. But a lot of that activity is moving
from bank reserves to treasury bills, or not something that's a real risk transformation.
Whereas here, we're talking about an entity that is investing substantial sums in large,
risky projects such as Ukraine, the Strait of Hormuz. We'll get all into that. But please,
I hope you enjoy this conversation with the Conor Coleman of the Development Finance Corporation.
Got a very special conversation. I'm joined by Conor Coleman, Chief of Staff and Head of
Investments at the Development Finance Corporation. Conor, welcome to monetary matters. Good to
see you. Jack, thank you so much for having me on today. I think it's really important, Conor,
because the DFC Development Finance Corporation does so much in finance, and many people are familiar
with the heavyweights, the Federal Reserve, BlackRock. But I think very few people, even
who work in the investment business, are no about the DFC. So first off, my first question,
what is the Development Finance Corporation? I think that's such a good point.
Frankly, I've been in the private sector my entire career before I took this opportunity,
and I didn't even hear about the DFC until then got the call from the president. By the way,
at background, the DFC is the international investment arm of the United States government,
and a central player to economic state craft for the United States. We look to drive private
capital mobilization through our investment strategy, which is both strategic investments,
and investments that drive economic development. Our goal is to make a return for the United States
taxpayer, drive US foreign policy initiatives, and achieve economic development and create
economic ecosystem in our partner nations. Just to give people a scale, the previous
amount of money that the Development Finance Corporation could invest was $60 billion. That's
just got raised to $205 billion. What are the rough asset classes that the overseas investment
authority of the United States government is investing in? Could they be categorized as loans,
as private equity, anything, you know, public stocks? What are we talking about here?
Under Ben's leadership, we're really kind of expanding our toolkit in order to
affectuate the message. So historically speaking, dating back to our predecessor agency OPEC,
and during the first couple of years of the DFC until we were reauthorized, we kind of focus on
investing through three main asset classes. One is senior secure project finance lending, which
is just senior secure loans. The second is offer providers of political risk insurance or guarantee
products. And the third is what I call intermediary investing, which is us being a traditional LPN funds
or lending into regional banks who then in turn led a country. I would say under Ben's leadership
and after the reauthorization, our toolkit has been expanded. So you're going to start seeing us
playing much more in equity investment, both common equity and structure equity. You're going to
see us utilizing the full suite of our loan products. So not just senior secured, but also
mezzanine and structured notes. Definitely going to still be providers of political risk insurance
and guarantee products, but I think expanding on those capabilities and really trying to utilize
in the new creative ways, you are starting to see us doing that with kind of the maritime insurance
product for the history of four moves with our insurance. So I think the name of the game right
now in this new DFC is being more creative, acting more like a traditional Wall Street finance
institution across the board. So you talk about the chief executive officer of the DFC Ben Black,
just to give people a sense that when people hear the Ukraine Minerals deal that President Trump did,
that was through the DFC when people talk about the straight-of-hormones reinsurance program,
that was through the DFC. So I want to get into those specific deals. Maybe let's start with the
reinsurance program. What is the goal of that deal? How did it meet the objectives of the DFC
and how's it going? I would say the maritime insurance product and just the way we structured
the investment and how quickly it came to fruition is really a great case study of how we're
going to be acting with the new DFC going forward. So as you probably saw in the president's true
social, he tasked the DFC to partner with the US military to create a product that offered the
ability to restart maritime commerce in the Gulf. So when we looked into that initiative and
really what it took to restart maritime commerce, it was two things. One, it was financial stability
because as you saw the market kind of pull back of offering war risk insurance during that time,
and then secondarily, it was operational security because when we looked into what was going on
in the Gulf, we realized that the captain of the ship is the sole person that can decide that a
ship is going to sail or not. It's not the owner of a ship, it's not the company that leased the
ship to move its cargo. The captain has full discretion over the ship and the safety of its crew.
So you had to offer a joint product, one that had the financial stability and the operational
stability, and that's why we came out in partnership with the US military. I would say on the
financial side of things, we were able to create a very unique reinsurance product, something we
did previously during the wartime in Ukraine, but now it's just done on a much larger scale.
We partnered with CHUB, it was the lead insurer, and we offered about 50% reinsurance to
for various different policies that were needed in the market, mainly whole machinery, cargo,
and a policy called PNI. So from a financial perspective, we were able to get the structure set up
in a month, which is the fastest executed deal in DFC's history. Obviously, there has been some
developments, and it changes on a daily basis, what is going on on the operational security side
with the straight-of-formos? We have been in close contact with the interagency on a daily basis.
I think the product itself is going to potentially launch and come online,
was it coming days or weeks? However, we're just waiting for that signal from the United States
military that the operational security could be achieved, because we don't want to turn it on.
If we can't be confident that we can provide operational security to the private sector.
I think of the Federal Reserve when the price of oil goes up or down. The Federal Reserve
controls a very specific set of things within the financial sector. They can't control a lot of
things without the remit. You're controlling the reinsurance, but there's a lot of other stuff
going through through the straight-of-formos, so that operational thing as well, in order to be
qualified for the reinsurance, they have to be escorted by the US Navy, and that's an issue.
That's what you're referring to, right?
I mean, look from the financial side of the things, we were able to get the structure out.
We have a portal that is ready to launch, so we're very proud of what we were able to do and move
from that financial structuring standpoint. I think this idea of creative financial structures
to solve massive geopolitical issues is something that is going to be consistent
theme under Ben's leadership, just given his experience in the private sector of doing that,
and having success doing that. Tell us about the Ukraine minerals deal.
So, it started off as purely a minerals deal, but I really want to highlight that it's turned
into a broader reconstruction fund across the board, so not only does the Europe, which we call
the Ukraine Reconstruction Investment Fund, Investing Critical Minerals, it also invests in infrastructure,
energy, and emergent technology across the board. So, it is a great example of basically the speed
of execution and what DFC was able to do. So, the Europe was able to not only be operational wise
in under nine months, but also make its first investment in under the year. So, it's fully set up,
it's operating within Ukraine. It's a joint partnership between the United States government
and the Ukrainian government, and it's actively making investments within Ukraine now.
All with the lens of how can you bring in the private sector along this investment?
We've realized what the value of Europe really is, is a signal to the market that you're going to
enjoy protection from both the Ukraine government and the United States government, and it shows a
commitment of both the countries to one, a lasting peace within the country, but to the future
reconstruction efforts. So, you put in 75 million alongside the Ukrainian government which put in
75 million. So, you have a 150 million in seed capital. Explain what that money is doing. Is it
investing in, is it buying existing minds? Is it launching new minds and also explain how you
want the partnership to work with the private sector? Yeah, and again, it's not just critical minerals,
the variety of different sectors, and it's actually a little bit over a hundred and just
a million right now because the vehicle is structured as a perpetual capital vehicle that is
going to be replenished with royalty streams from kind of greenfield hydrocarbon, critical
minerals, energy products within Ukraine. So, we're already starting to see some of those royalty
streams come in and it's going to scale over time. So, the model of the fund is to provide debt
or equity investments into companies within Ukraine. We made our first investment in actually
emergent technology called, they called signed technologies which offers components to certain
drone capabilities there. So, that is one of the examples. I think we have a pipeline right now
of deals that we have looked at. It have been submitted to Europe of over 200. I expect probably
for us to make probably a three to four investment, more investments this year alone. And then we
are also looking at how do we add to the firepower of Europe. We're looking at potentially rolling
up co-investment vehicles with the private sector for certain key products like political risk
insurance, which was a lot of the private sector is asking for for their ability to invest in
Ukraine, especially during a conflict period. That's happening right now as well as key sectors
like emergent technology or energy across the board. So, we want to bring in our partner,
both kind of quasi multilateral rules and public partners as well as the private sector,
sovereign wealth funds, alternative asset managers to invest alongside of us.
Thank you for explaining. It's not just critical minerals, but if we can focus on the natural resources
part, you know, Ukraine has a vast array of natural minerals, not just rare earths. Is the
fund like buying existing mines or is it funding new development of those mines?
So, it's both. So, it can be green field projects or it can be brown field projects. So,
we're looking at both different asset classes. So, we're not limited with with the
ability of what we can invest in. I got it. And so, who is is Ben the point man? Are you the point
man? Look, everything runs through Ben at the end of the day. He is the CEO of the agency. He's
tasked me with some of the day-to-day operations of Europe, especially because I was in the seat
a little bit before Ben, because Ben was had to be semi-confirmed. So, while we were going through
some of the negotiations and kind of the structuring of the fund, I was the the one in the building
structure and yet, but he has definitely high visibility into it. He signs up for all the key
decisions. He just the lead the day-to-day operational to myself and the team. We have here at
the DFC, which I have to give a shout out to his absolutely fantastic with their with what
they've been able to achieve in such a short time frame. So, you said it's slightly over 150
million, just roughly how much have you invested so far and how much kind of you have left in
in dry powder. And then also when you said those royalty streams or those royalty things,
were those things that you bought at the beginning of the deal?
So, two things. One, when you're talking about our our amount, we haven't able to deploy.
I think we have the majority of our capital still ready to deploy. Again, we've only made one
investment to date. I think our pipeline shows we'll eat into that 150 million very, very quickly.
So, I'm not worried about the deployment there, or there were also looking from the DFC side of
things of how can we augment additional capital direct into projects, but we have a much larger
balance sheet than what fun does. So, that's what we are very excited about to get explored here.
That makes sense. Explain the the mandate of the DFC. You know, you go on DFC, it says critical
minerals, national security, healthcare, explain are those all equal weights or is, you know,
I clearly was a large focus on natural natural resources. And then I also have questions about
the degree you're trying to make a return for, you know, not shareholders, but that the US taxpayer
versus meeting the operational goals of the US government. Certainly, there are many instances
where there's a line, but certainly there's issues where they are never as well.
At the DFC, we don't deploy capital for the sake of deploying capital. Obviously, we have to
drive US foreign policy goals and objectives through our investment thesis. So, we really look
to where the United States government wants us to be playing just from a sector and geography
standpoint, and then really fold that into our investment thesis. So, on a sector as you are
named a couple, but just to walk you through our seven key sectors is critical minerals.
It's transportation and the logistic infrastructure. So, think ports, airports, railways, energy,
technology and telecommunications, food security and agriculture, healthcare supply chain,
and financial services. So, those are the main areas we're going to look to to invest. Obviously,
from administration priority standpoint, you've seen the rhetoric in the news. I would say the
the first four I mentioned, critical minerals infrastructure and technology and telecommunications
and energy are really kind of some of the key driving areas that we are going to be focused on
and where you're going to see our probably bigger ticket items. However, the other key areas,
like food security and agriculture, healthcare supply chain, financial services are really
important to build around these large infrastructure or large investments to create economic ecosystems
within our in our partner countries. So, we really feel that creating economic markets is what
economic development is in today's world. Additionally, when we think about our priority geographies,
we're going to focus on Latin America and the Caribbean just given that as a massive focus of
protecting your own backyard. I would say Central Asia, Eastern Europe and the Indo-Pacific
is very important to this administration. And then Africa's 25% of my portfolio currently,
and I expect that to be heavily invested there too. I think poster reauthorization that happened
this past December, we've really changed our investment mold and model from being a traditional
development finance institution that just has catalytic concessional financing to really more of
a strategic investment arm. Because you're right, not every deal, I'm looking for just solely
a financial return. Obviously, I have to make a return for the U.S. taxpayer, but there are
certain investments where I have a high impact, whether that be strategic investments or economic
development investments across the board. So, we are going to have a concessional transformational
portfolio that focuses on highly impactful investments, but we're also going to have a very
commercially driven portfolio that focuses on driving private capital mobilization making that
return. So, on a net basis, I'm not only making a very strategic impact for the U.S. taxpayer,
I'm also making a financial return for that. I should say that in your reauthorization, 90% of the
$205 billion investment cap maximum contingent liability has to be used in countries that are not
wealthy or high-income countries. So, you talked about a senior, if you talked about Latin
America, what kind of counts as a wealthy high-income country and what counts as a non-wealthy,
you know, high-income countries? So, we do have two limitations on reaching the best in wealthy
countries. One is my portfolio at any one moment can't be more than $20 billion in the wealthy
countries and I can't invest more than 25% of the total project cost into these, into these
wealthy countries. What defines a wealthy country? Actually, look to the World Bank index. So,
we look at the World Bank GDP for the calculation of what the wealthy country, what's an advancing
income country, and what's a low to middle income country as well. So, it's tied via our statute
to this index, which obviously changes on a yearly basis as countries move in and out of
across the board, but it's tied to the World Bank one. Got it. That makes sense. I think, do you have
any overlap at all between something like the World Bank or the IMF, which is known for making loans
to low-income countries, but I think a lot of them are sovereign loans, but there are some infrastructure
loans. I wonder if there's ever, you know, some overlap there? With the World Bank and the IFC
absolutely, we have a ton of overlap with them. We work very closely with that of the World Bank,
as well as kind of our other multilateral development finance institutions across the board.
I do think a goal of ours is to not only partner with just the multilateral development finance
institution, but really start partnering as well with the alternative asset managers with the
sovereign wealth funds given our new authority. So, you're going to start seeing play in that area as
well. The IMF is one we don't necessarily have a ton of overlap with given they are more sovereign
loans and looking at that trading, but obviously, the Department of Treasury here in the US government
is our board member. They're very involved in the IMF as well. So, there is a lot of overlap
in in terms of kind of policy thinking about what happens between our two organizations.
Hope you're enjoying today's interview. This episode of Monetary Matters is brought to you by the
Tuchrium Corn Fund ticker CORN. If you follow the show, you know we spend a lot of time on macro themes
like energy transitions, geopolitical risk, and global food security. Corn sits at the intersection
of all three. Most people watching the straight of her moves are focused on oil. They should be looking
at nitrogen. A third of the world's fertilizer trade passes through that choke point. When that
corridor tightens, fertilizer prices react, and corn farmers feel it first. Corn is the heaviest
nitrogen user in US agriculture, so rising input costs hit their margins quickly. The longer term
story could cut the other way too. If farmers pull back on fertilizer application yields may come down.
If the economics get difficult enough, some may choose to abandon corn and plant something else
entirely. Either scenario potentially tightens corn supply, which could be price supportive.
Bottom line, the pinch on the producer side could become the price story on the commodity side.
Tuchrium's corn ETF gives you exposure to corn prices, ticker CORN, traded on NYSE ARCA,
access it through your brokerage account, no futures account required. Tuchrium also has a family
of agricultural ETFs, including the Tuchrium Wheat Fund ticker WEAT, the Tuchrium Soybean Fund ticker SOYB,
and the Tuchrium Sugar Fund ticker C-A-N-E. Head to Tuchrium.com to learn more that's T-E-U-C-R-I-U-M.com.
This material must be preceded or accompanied by a perspective. The perspective is available
at Tuchrium.com slash CORN. Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives,
risks, charges, and expenses of the fund before investing. The perspective contains this and
other important information. Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principle.
Commodities and futures generally are volatile, and instruments whose underlying investments include
commodities and futures are now suitable for all investors. Pass performance is not guarantee
future results. Thanks for listening, let's get back to today's interview. Tell us about the
relationship between you and the other private investors,
alternative asset management firms you mentioned. Are you looking to co-invest with them,
be senior to them, and why do you want to partner with them? Taking a step back,
when you think about what the DFC was created for in 2019, under the first Trump administration,
it was to be the counterweight to China's one belt one road initiative. When you look at China
and what they're doing to an economic activity standpoint, I think last year they invested
$125 billion in a one belt one road, which is more than half of my total AUM and investment capacity.
Obviously, they have a different economic strategy than we do, and as they should be,
I don't think we should ever fall the mold of what China is doing from an investment standpoint.
But our goal, if we really are at compete, is how do we bridge the gap? That's really with
utilizing the United States' greatest strength, which is our private sector and our capital
markets institution. So our goal when we partner with the private sector is really to be a multiplier
effect. How can every dollar I invest really lead to two, three, four, five X private sector
dollars coming out alongside of me? In order to do that, especially when you start playing some of
these emerging market geographies where maybe certain of the alternative asset managers and
the sovereign wealth funds do not have a ton of investment experience, you have to be flexible
with your capital. So I'm not always going to be a senior secured lender, I'm not always going to
be an equity holder. My role is to understand where does the private sector want to invest? Where do
they feel most comfortable earning your way of return? Where's public sector dollars? And where is
that going to play in the capital structure? And then I'm supposed to be that part of the capital
structure that really makes everything pencil. So that's what we're really trying to still hear.
You have to see is wait, listen, and be really creative when thinking of the financing structure
and have us be the flexible one instead of having these established just like products that we're
only going to push because that's what really it's going to take to mobilize private capital on scale.
So it sounds like you guys have a lot of flexibility. Yes, and that's the fun thing.
Yes, that is. Tell us one deal that is one of the biggest deals you've done this year is Orion,
which is $100 million in equity investment as well as $500 million in debt. I wonder if you
just focus on that. The Orion CMC is another example of this creative financing structure that we're
looking to deploy. So historically speaking, we were just kind of a traditional LP and funds.
Now we are moving more towards a partnership joint venture model and CMC is an example of this
type of investment strategy. So this vehicle is in partnership with Orion in ADQ. As of today,
each of us has invested $600 million into the joint partnership and it's for the purposes of
investing in critical minerals upstream and midstream in international markets. I think it's also
very important to highlight that us at the DFC, we have certain veto rights regarding this
on this vehicle. So we're not going to invest in every project. We want to make sure it aligns
with US policy priorities as well as we're making sure we have controls around kind of where the
off take goes to making sure it's going to the United States or allied or allied partners.
I think it's a really creative example of how can you still have control direct the investment
thesis, but utilize the private sector's knowledge and resources. Orion has been able to invest
in this critical mineral space at scale for over the past 20 years earning kind of a very high
return for all that's fun. So you want to utilize the expertise and know-how to deploy capital quickly
to achieve some of these administrative priorities. And when it comes to mining, would you ever partner
with like a publicly traded mining company or have a sort of structure with them or would it have
to be something that is private equity or something in the private markets? No, we would absolutely
partnered with public companies. I think I'll point to two examples of this that we're already
doing so. So one, we just did our first ever kind of debt for equity transaction from an existing
resident to restructure and we actually ended up taking an equity stake in a publicly traded
company called Sura, which is Australian listed. It has a mine in Mozambique as well as a processing
facility within the, within the United States. So that is an example of us being very proactive
to obscure the supply chain and actually play in some public markets. And then we had an investment
in Sarah Verde that is actually just announced a potential merger with USA Rare Earth, which is a
publicly traded company here in the United States. Obviously, if that merger goes through, we would be
playing in a public company as well too. So I would say across all of our sectors, not just critical
minerals, we're going to work to play in the public public side of things every once in a while.
Would you ever do something in the semiconductor supply chain or something to do with AI? Of course,
that is again, another big administration priority is how can we export the US tech stack? We've
been working very closely with our commerce department, with our state department, and the White House
who are really leading this initiative to figure out how can we fold in the companies, the technologies
they want to see exported abroad into our investment pieces. Does everything have to be approved by
the board member? Because I look at the board. It's pretty stacked, you know, commerce secretary
Lutnik, secretary treasury, Scott Besson, secretary of state, Marco Rubio. What's the role of the board?
Is it any investment decisions or just the overall mission? Look, I would say we definitely have
a very stacked board, which is, which is great because especially in this administration, our board
has experience investing at scale on the private sector. They definitely have a lot of views, and they
have been extremely helpful to us across the board with thinking through how to deploy our capital.
I would say not every transaction goes to board. We have certain delegate authority amounts,
so that our CEO can just approve transactions below a certain threshold. But when transactions
do go to board, obviously, they're going to have comments, they're going to have thoughts on the
structure. But really, the board is really to help us drive our policy priorities, think about our
capital allocation strategy, figure out how we're to deploy our capital amongst our different sectors
and help us build to that investment thesis. And then, personally, when I think about the utility
of our board, especially to our private sector partners, it's more so in the portfolio management
stage of our investment because we have access to all of these different agencies. We work extremely
closely with them. Then I have great relationships with each of them, so we can bring them
into investments to make sure we're making the rate of return of the private sector wants,
and generating the impact that our investments are expected throughout the life cycle of our investments.
So do you think 2026 is going to be a big year for the DFC?
Absolutely. I think the next three years are going to be massive year for the DFC.
Well, when you think about where we are AUM-Ys and where our investment capacity at $25
billion dollars is over the next six years, that's an average deployment of $27.5 billion,
which makes us one of the largest liquidity providers globally. And I track our pipeline on our
weekly basis. I know it's coming down the pike and I have no worries that we're going to hit
at the deployment of now over the coming years under Ben's leadership.
If someone is looking to invest in the rest of the world, they should reach out to Conor.
What are you looking for in terms of a deal? What do you say, oh my god, I got to bring this to Ben
versus something that's like, you know, I'm going to pass on this. I don't even have to tell Ben about
this. I just know that it's a no. Well, I would say what we are really focused on here and I think
you're already seeing the play out with our investment is getting a great financial return
and making a massive impact is not mutually exclusive. I think that's a huge mischaracterization
on investment institutions like us, like it's either one or the other. I think what we've been
able to prove out given our private sector connections, given our relationships is like you can
achieve a great financial return for the taxpayer and making massive impact whether in the host
country or for the United States overall with the investment. So what really excites me is when
an investment drives us foreign policy, creating massive impact in the host country that we're
investing in and makes a great financial return for the US taxpayer. So that's really where we're
trying to drive a lot of our pipeline to achieve all three of those goals in one investment.
We'll leave it there Connor. Thank you so much. Jack, thank you very much. I appreciate
it. I'll look you forward to the next time. Me too. Thanks. I hope you enjoyed today's episode.
Those interested in learning more about the Tuquillium corn fund ticker C-O-R-N can find more
information in the link in the description. Until next time.
